examples.title: Basic File Operations with Error Handling
examples.description: Demonstrates opening, reading from a file with proper error checking using errno and perror().
examples.explanation: This program demonstrates fundamental Unix system programming concepts: open() for file access, read() for input, write() for output, and close() to release resources. Every system call is checked for -1 return value, and perror() displays both the custom message and the errno-based error description.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) - fixed buffer size regardless of input

examples.title: Understanding File Descriptors and Standard Streams
examples.description: Demonstrates the three standard file descriptors (STDIN, STDOUT, STDERR) used in Unix.
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates direct use of file descriptors rather than streams (FILE*). write() is a system call that outputs raw bytes. Notice how STDERR (2) remains unbuffered and visible even when STDOUT is redirected - a key Unix design principle for error reporting.

examples.title: Error Handling with errno Values
examples.description: Shows explicit errno checking using strerror() for detailed error handling.
examples.explanation: This program shows sophisticated error handling: strerror() converts errno to human-readable string, and switch statement handles specific errors differently. This pattern allows programs to recover gracefully from different failure scenarios rather than treating all errors the same.
